fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out the patch management toolkit we've been talking about. The basic idea is that devs can declare patches for their third-party dependencies and the system handles everything automatically — fetching, verifying, applying, all of it. We want this to feel like a first-class part of the dependency workflow, not a bolt-on.

A few things I know we need: patches should be describable as little records with a source location and some metadata, and those records need to survive being saved and reloaded (think lock file round-trips). We also need to group patches by which package they target and make sure the same patch doesn't get applied twice — remember how we handled the deduplication logic in the login module? Something similar here, but patches can collide in more than one way.

Patches also need to be applied at the right 'level' in the directory tree — some well-known packages have special requirements here, and there's a fallback chain similar to what we discussed in the config resolution spike. The system should support declaring patches either inline in the project config or in a separate file, and that file path situation needs to handle missing or misconfigured files gracefully without blowing up.

We also need the lock file naming to follow the active manifest name. And the download step should verify file integrity and report clearly when something doesn't match. Finally the patching tool itself should check whether it's even usable in the current environment before trying anything.

trigger_keywords: what fields does a patch record have
what metadata does a patch carry
what is in a patch record
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A patch record has three required fields: package (target package name), description (human-readable label), and url (source location, local path or remote URL). It also has three optional fields: sha256 (content hash), depth (apply strip depth, integer), and extra (a metadata map that defaults to an empty list/array when absent). Unset optional fields serialize as null in the wire form.

trigger_keywords: how does deduplication work
when are two patches considered the same
what counts as a duplicate patch
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Two patches for the same package collide — and the later one is discarded — in two distinct cases: (1) they share the exact same source URL, or (2) they share the same sha256 content hash even if their URLs differ (for example, one URL may carry a fragment suffix like #something). The earliest-added patch always wins.

trigger_keywords: how is apply depth determined
what depth is used when none is specified
depth resolution order
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The effective apply depth follows a strict precedence chain: (1) an explicit depth set on the patch record itself wins first; (2) then a per-package override from the project configuration under the 'package-depths' key; (3) then a built-in default for well-known packages (e.g. drupal/core has a built-in default of 2); (4) finally the global fallback default of 1 is used when nothing else matches.

trigger_keywords: what built-in package depths exist
which packages have a default depth
drupal/core depth
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: drupal/core is a known well-known package with a built-in default apply depth of 2. Any package not in the built-in list returns null for its default depth lookup.

trigger_keywords: how can patches be declared
what formats are supported for declaring patches
inline vs file patches
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Patches can be declared in two ways: (1) inline in the root project configuration as a per-package map, supporting both an expanded form (list of objects each with url and description) and a compact form (a description-to-url map); (2) in a dedicated external patches file referenced by the project, which must contain a top-level 'patches' key grouping patches by package name.

trigger_keywords: what happens when the patches file is missing
how are patches file errors handled
what errors can the patches file produce
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A missing patches file path or an unconfigured (absent) path is silently ignored and contributes no patches. A file whose JSON parses successfully but contains no top-level 'patches' key is rejected with a normalized error category of 'no_patches_found'. A file with malformed JSON (fails to parse) is rejected with a normalized error category of 'invalid_json'.

trigger_keywords: how is the lock file named
what is the lock file name
lock file naming convention
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default lock file name is 'patches.lock.json'. When the active project manifest is overridden to a custom filename (e.g. via an environment variable 'manifest_env'), the lock file name is derived by taking the base name of that manifest (without the .json extension) and appending '-patches.lock.json'. For example, a manifest named 'mycomposer.json' produces a lock file named 'mycomposer-patches.lock.json'.

trigger_keywords: how does download verification work
what happens on a hash mismatch
how is file integrity checked
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a patch is fetched, its sha256 content hash is computed. If no expected hash was supplied, the computed hash is recorded and the local copy is marked as present. If an expected hash was supplied and matches the computed hash, the fetch succeeds normally. If the expected hash does not match the actual computed hash, the operation fails with a normalized error of 'hash_mismatch', reporting both the expected and actual hash values.

trigger_keywords: how does the patcher check if it can run
what tool does the patcher use
patcher availability check
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The patcher probes its backing tool (git) by attempting to run it. The patcher reports itself as available only when the tool is present on the system and executes successfully. It is unavailable when the configured tool path points to a missing executable or when the tool exits with a failure code. The tool name reported is 'git'.

trigger_keywords: how does collection serialization work
what does the wire form of a collection look like
collection round trip format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The collection serializes to JSON with a top-level 'patches' key whose value is an object mapping each package name to an array of full patch records. Each record in the serialized form always includes all fields: package, description, url, sha256 (null if unset), depth (null if unset), and extra (empty array [] if unset). The JSON is formatted with 4-space indentation.

trigger_keywords: what order are packages listed in the collection
insertion order for packages
how are patched packages ordered
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Packages in the collection are tracked and listed in the order they were first seen (insertion order). The patched_packages listing respects this insertion order.

trigger_keywords: what happens when a package is removed from the collection
how does clearing a package work
remove all patches for a package
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Removing (clearing) a package from the collection drops all of its patches at once and leaves all other packages completely untouched. After removal, querying the cleared package returns a count of 0, and it no longer appears in the patched_packages list.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'patch_wire', 'collection_lifecycle', 'collection_dedupe', 'collection_wire', 'default_depth', 'guess_depth', 'resolve_root', 'resolve_file', 'lock_file_path', 'download_local', 'patcher_availability'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: deduplication logic similar to the login module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Patch deduplication in the collection layer: two patches collide if they share the same URL OR share the same sha256 hash (even with different URLs), as specified in feature2_2_collection_deduplication.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: fallback chain similar to what we discussed in the config resolution spike
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The depth precedence chain: patch-explicit depth > project config 'package-depths' override > built-in well-known package defaults (e.g. drupal/core=2) > global default of 1, as specified in feature3_2_depth_precedence.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: the lock file naming to follow the active manifest name
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Lock file naming logic in feature5_lock_file_name.json: default is 'patches.lock.json', but when manifest_env is set to e.g. 'mycomposer.json', the lock file becomes 'mycomposer-patches.lock.json'

context_pointers.vague_reference: patches can collide in more than one way
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Two collision conditions: (1) identical source URL, (2) identical sha256 hash regardless of URL differences such as fragment suffixes, per feature2_2_collection_deduplication.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect handy — you'll want to dig into the existing codebase and test cases to figure out the exact behavior expected there. Check the feature test JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the concrete input/output contracts.

feature1_patch_serialization.json.description: A patch record carries a target package name, a human description, a source URL, and optional fields (content hash, apply depth, and an extra metadata map). Serializing a record to its wire form and parsing it back yields an identical record. Unset optional fields appear as null in the wire form and remain unset after a round trip; the extra map defaults to an empty object. The output lists each field of the record reconstructed from its own serialized wire form.

cases.expected_output:
package=drupal/drupal
description=Test description
url=https://google.com
sha256=asdf
depth=0
extra=[]

package=some/package
description=only required fields
url=https://example.com/x.patch
sha256=null
depth=null
extra=[]

feature2_1_collection_grouping.json.description: Patches are added to a collection and grouped by their target package. Querying a package returns the count of patches stored for it, and the collection can list every package that currently has patches in insertion order. Removing a package drops all of its patches at once, leaving other packages untouched. The output reports per-package counts and the list of patched packages before and after a removal.

cases.expected_output:
some/package patches=2
other/package patches=2
patched_packages=some/package,other/package
cleared=other/package
other/package patches=0
patched_packages=some/package

feature2_2_collection_deduplication.json.description: When two patches for the same package would collide, the collection keeps only the first and discards later duplicates. Two patches collide when they share the same source URL, or when they share the same content hash even if their URLs differ (for example one carries a URL fragment). The output shows the surviving patch count for the package and the descriptions that remain, demonstrating that the earliest-added patch wins.

feature2_3_collection_serialization.json.description: A whole collection can be serialized to a wire form that nests, under each target package, the full record of every patch (including null placeholders for unset optional fields and an empty extra map). Parsing that wire form back reproduces an equivalent collection. The output is the canonical wire form of a collection rebuilt from its own serialization, grouping patches under their package names.

feature3_1_default_depth.json.description: Certain well-known packages have a built-in default apply depth that is used when a patch does not specify its own depth. Looking up a package returns its known default depth, or a null result for any package that is not in the built-in list. The output echoes the queried package and the resolved default depth.

feature3_2_depth_precedence.json.description: The effective apply depth for a patch is resolved through a precedence chain: an explicit depth on the patch itself wins first; otherwise a per-package override from the project configuration is used; otherwise a built-in default for well-known packages applies; otherwise the global default depth of 1 is used. The output echoes the patch's target package (or null when the patch names no package) and the resolved depth.

feature4_1_root_config_patches.json.description: Patches can be declared directly in the root project configuration under a per-package map. Each package may use the expanded form (a list of objects with url and description) or the compact form (a description-to-url map). Resolving the configuration produces a collection grouping the declared patches by package. An empty declaration yields no patches. The output reports per-package patch counts and the list of patched packages.

feature4_2_patches_file.json.description: Patches can also be declared in an external patches file referenced by the project. A valid file groups patches by package and resolves to a collection with the expected per-package counts. A file whose JSON parses but contains no patches key is rejected with a no_patches_found error; a file with malformed JSON is rejected with an invalid_json error. A file path that does not exist, or an unconfigured (empty) path, is silently ignored and adds nothing. The output reports either the normalized error category or the per-package counts and patched-package list.

feature5_lock_file_name.json.description: The patches lock file is named after the active project manifest. With the default manifest the lock file is named patches.lock.json; when the active manifest is overridden to a custom name, the lock file name is derived by appending -patches.lock.json to that manifest's base name. The output reports the resolved lock file name.

feature6_download_and_verify.json.description: A patch is fetched from a local source into a working location, and its content hash (sha256) is computed. When no expected hash is supplied, the computed hash is recorded and the local copy is available. When an expected hash is supplied and matches, the fetch succeeds. When the expected hash does not match the actual content, the operation fails with a normalized hash_mismatch error reporting the expected and actual hashes. The output reports either the computed hash and local-file availability, or the normalized mismatch error with both hashes.

feature7_patcher_availability.json.description: A patcher reports whether it can be used in the current environment by probing its backing tool. The patcher is available only when the tool is present and runs successfully; it is unavailable when the configured tool path points to a missing executable or to a tool that exits with a failure. The output reports the tool name and whether it is available.
